column1: Here is a comparative table of magical prince, frog prince, serpent prince, unicorn, and princess stories and folktales from around the world, focusing on tales where a prince is transformed, hidden, or magical, and is united with a princess. The table includes summary, source/collection (with detail), attributed year (origin in oral tradition when possible), publication/recording date, location, culture, and language.

column2: Princess promises to befriend a frog who retrieves her ball; frog transforms into prince after trials
column3: Grimm, "The Frog King or Iron Heinrich"6911

column2: Youngest daughter marries a serpent who is a prince; she burns his skin, must endure trials to win him back
Youngest daughter marries a serpent; after burning his skin, she endures magical trials to find him
Prince cursed to be a serpent; princess must break spell, often with magical animal help
Princess summons magical prince with a fan; sisters injure him, she undertakes quest to heal him
Princess receives a box with a magical fan; summons prince, faces trials after sisters’ betrayal
Princess must break enchantment on a sleeping prince; false bride motif

column1: The Magic Prince and the Enchanted Forest
column2: Prince with magical powers helps others in enchanted land
Prince marries a frog who becomes a princess; transformation and magical trials

column1: East of the Sun, West of the Moon
column2: Girl marries a bear (enchanted prince); must rescue him from trolls after breaking taboo
Girl falls in love with a beast, who is a cursed prince
Girl marries a black bull (enchanted prince); must undertake quest to win him back
Unicorn helps or transforms for a princess; motifs of purity, rescue, or magical union
Prince completes arduous quest, often magical, to win princess
Three sisters; youngest promises wonders, becomes queen, is betrayed, son becomes hero.
column3: Pushkin, "The Tale of Tsar Saltan"; ATU 707

column2: Three sisters; youngest promises hero, betrayal, sea exile, magical reunification.
Three sisters, youngest promises miraculous sons, betrayal, sea exile, magical reunion.
Three sisters, youngest promises golden-haired sons, betrayal, magical rescue, reunion.

column1: The Three Sisters and Their Glass Hearts
column2: Three princesses with glass hearts; vulnerability and transformation.
Three sisters compete in trials to win a husband; youngest succeeds.
Three sisters, youngest succeeds, often marries prince.

column1: The Death of Koschei the Deathless
column2: King with three daughters, magical dangers, magical helpers.
Three sisters, youngest marries serpent king, faces trials, transformation.
Hero must obtain three golden hairs, passes three trials, wins princess.

column1: The Goat and Her Three Kids
column2: Three siblings, mother warns, wolf devours careless ones, youngest survives.
Simplicity and fortune; golden egg, loss, and acceptance.

column1: Prince Sobur / The Fan Prince / Prince Sabar
column2: Youngest of seven sisters expelled, magical fan brings prince, sisters' jealousy, reunion.
Poor boy acquires magic lamp, becomes wealthy, faces trials, wins princess.
Humble origins, trials, magical aid, rise to kingship or wealth.
Young Arthur pulls sword from stone, proves royal lineage, becomes king.

column1: The Legend of the Three Sisters (Iroquois)
column2: Three agricultural sisters (corn, beans, squash) represent unity and interdependence.

column1: The Prince and the Silver Rabbit
column2: King with three daughters, magical dangers, prince as helper, rags-to-riches.

column3: Faces monsters, demons, betrayal, impossible tasks
Betrays and kills rightful king Jamshid; rules tyrannically, overthrown by Fereydun
Hunted by Zahhak, hidden as a child, returns to overthrow tyrant
Refuses to endorse Zahhak, leads revolt to restore rightful king
Born with white hair, abandoned, raised by Simorgh, returns to claim place

column3: Conspire to overthrow evil usurper Zahhak
Hidden as a child to escape assassination, raised in obscurity, later revealed
Betrayed and murdered by Cambyses; impostor (Gaumata) takes throne
Plots with six nobles to overthrow the Magian usurper (Gaumata)

Paranoid ruler, fratricide, self-destruction, destabilizes kingdom

column5: Recognized the God of Daniel as "the living God" whose kingdom will never end

column5: Instrument of God’s plan, though not directly messianic

column5: Used as instrument of judgment; not messianic

column7: Transitioning from tribal confederacy to monarchy.
Established Jerusalem as capital, expanded territory.
Built the First Temple, known for wisdom, but also foreign wives and cults.
After Solomon's death, the kingdom divided.
Most kings of Israel are portrayed as wicked; Kings of Judah varied, some righteous, some not.
Appointed governor by Cyrus the Great, oversaw rebuilding of the Second Temple.

column6: Ezra, Nehemiah, Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews (Book XI)
column7: High Priests held considerable power, but under Persian/later Hellenistic control.

column6: 1 Maccabees 2; Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews (Book XII)
column7: Led the Maccabean revolt against Seleucid rule and Hellenistic practices.

column6: 1 Maccabees 3-9; Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews (Book XII)
column7: Continued the revolt, rededicated the Second Temple.

column6: 1 Maccabees 10-12; Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews (Book XIII)
column7: Negotiated with Seleucids, became High Priest and de facto ruler.

column6: 1 Maccabees 13-16; Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews (Book XIII)
column7: Gained full independence from Seleucids, hereditary High Priest and ruler.

column6: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews (Book XIII)
column7: Expanded territory, forced conversions, increasingly Hellenized court.

column6: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews (Book XIII)
column7: First Hasmonean to take the title "king," embraced Hellenistic culture.

column6: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews (Book XIII)
column7: Expanded kingdom, brutally suppressed internal dissent, highly Hellenized.

column6: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews (Book XIII)
column7: Only female ruler of Judea, balanced Pharisees and Sadducees, period of relative peace.

column6: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews (Books XIII-XIV)
column7: Deposed by brother Aristobulus, later restored by Romans.

column6: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews (Books XIII-XIV)
column7: Opposed Hyrcanus, briefly seized the throne, appealed to Romans.

column6: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews (Book XIV)
column7: With Parthian support, briefly ousted Hyrcanus and Herod, ultimately defeated by Romans.

column6: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews (Books XIV-XVII); Matthew 2:1-12
column7: Ruthless, rebuilt the Second Temple, massive building projects, heavily Hellenized court and administration.

column6: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews (Book XVII); Jewish War (Book II)
column7: Ruled Judea, Samaria, and Idumea; incompetent and deposed by Romans.

column6: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews (Book XVIII); Luke 3:19-20, 23:6-12
column7: Ruled Galilee and Perea; executed John the Baptist, involved in Jesus's trial.

column6: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews (Book XIX); Acts 12:1-25
column7: Briefly reunited much of Herod's kingdom, persecuted Christians, popular with Jews.

column6: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews (Books XX); Acts 25-26
column7: Last of the Herodians, sided with Romans during Jewish revolt.
